title: What will food be like in 15 - 45 years - Request

transcript.text: Hello, here's a video request from Mark Del Rio.
What will food be like in 15 to 45 years from now?
Interesting video request.
I think 15 to 45 years, food will be healthier.
I don't think people will be eating as much junk food,
Like the ice cream and the pie section and all that kind of stuff.
It won't be as large.
I think donuts and Twinkies and this kind of thing in the supermarket section
Will be a lot smaller.
People's cravings for that stuff will go away because they'll just generally be healthier.
I think that there won't be as many restaurants around.
I think people will be cooking for themselves a lot more than they are now.
I think that future, like, you know, because nowadays restaurants is a place
Where people get together, kind of, you know.
It's not just about eating nice prepared food.
It's about getting together.
But I think people in the future will get together more in houses.
I think right now people get together at restaurants
Because they just don't trust other people in their house.
They feel like their personal space is being violated,
So they organize, like, neutral places to go.
But I think in the future people will be sharing their houses a lot more
To strangers, you know, having, you know, meetings and all that kind of stuff.
Just sit at your house and stuff.
Whew!
A restaurant.
I think the space that restaurants take up now will be replaced with,
Well, that's a good question, with apartments and stuff.
Cities will get more, you know, densely populated in that regard.
And also parks.
There will be more parks, just open spaces.
And, you know, widening of car lanes and more bike lanes and stuff like that
Will take up places where there used to be buildings where there were restaurants.
In the supermarket, there will be a lot of, like, pre-fab meals.
You know, lasagna and pizzas and that kind of stuff.
Yeah.
But I think more and more people will just be eating really simply.
You know, like canned food.
Just doing what I do, just cracking up the canned food and eating it,
You know, are cooking up the oven meal in the recycled container.
The containers will be a lot more recycled.
And, you know, we pretty much recycle everything now.
You know, most of our garbage, well, I've been getting a lot of, like,
Pre-fab food at the supermarket because I just moved in.
Shoot the house.
Most of them I want to, you know, experiment, I want to experience all the different brands of food.
You know, I don't get all the different food that all the different companies offer,
But I'll choose one meal that one company offers and then move on to the next one.
Although there's one that I go back to, DiGiorno Supreme Pizza.
I love it, man.
It's like, it's so, they have a lot of tomato sauce in it.
And it's thick and filling, like, even when I'm totally exhausted.
I mean, starving.
I still can't finish a whole one of those pizzas by myself.
Yeah, and they're like $5.50 or something.
But other than that, yeah, just bounce around.
And then I'll go back to eating just out of the cans.
Although I have a refrigerator now, so I think I'm going to,
When that's done, I'll start eating more of the raw food.
You know, like, tomatoes are super cheap.
They're like $13.60 each or something.
So I'll start eating raw tomatoes.
And maybe breaking out, you know, salads.
Start having salads more.
Because the lettuce can keep and stuff.
You know, with spinach, you know.
Because they don't sell canned spinach.
Not that I saw in the supermarket.
The only thing I can get in the supermarket is, like, corn, peas, carrots, potatoes, and beans.
So for the cheap price.
So yeah, I'll get the raw, fresh vegetables, spinach.
I bought a lot of garlic, too.
I want to start frying up garlic.
Anyways, yeah, more and more people will be eating just, you know, raw food.
And not spend as much time cooking.
Nowadays, people like to spend, like, an hour cooking and stuff.
And I think in the future, a lot more people are just going to look at food as something to keep you alive.
You know, people eat to live.
They don't live to eat.
You know, a lot of people right now are like, oh, community gardens.
I saw a community garden actually just one block from my house.
I didn't even know it existed until a couple days ago.
They're all over the place.
But you don't have to, you know, do all that to have the nice cheap food.
Food will be really cheap in the future, man.
Once we start hooking up aeroponics, I think in 15 years, we'll start to realize that that's the best way to grow food.
And by 45 years, it'll really be kicking into gear right in the city.
It'll take away the expenses of commuting the food.
We'll have big buildings dedicated just to growing food with aeroponic.
If you go to aeroponics, it's actually invented right here in Boulder.
It's aeroponics, and it grows vegetables and stuff, flowers and all that, five times faster than the best dirt.
And they've had a little video comparison to show it.
And then the roots dangle out of the bottom and hang in a mist.
And you throw in, like, phosphorus vitamins once a week, and then you just plug it up, and that's it.
And you pour water in it, and it doesn't go through much water because it recycles it and doesn't let it evaporate.
And, you know, in that hermetically sealed chamber in there.
And then it's lit by a fluorescent light, which doesn't cost a whole lot of electricity.
In the future, we'll also hook up, you know, more renewable electricity, solar panels and stuff.
My building has a whole bunch of solar panels all over the roof of it.
So, I'm giving clues to exactly where I live, man.
If somebody watches all of my videos, they'll know exactly where I live.
So, yeah, so people will just be growing that stuff at those aeroponics.
Then they'll be canning it.
Or they won't even have to can it if it's grown in your hometown.
That'll be a thing of the past.
You'll just be able to buy the fresh vegetables and fruits and all that stuff at the supermarket.
So, then the can section will pretty much be very small, and the fresh produce section will be gigantic.
In the supermarket near my house, the produce section is not that big.
It's just a corner, you know.
But it's a supermarket.
And all the other sections with, like, the potato chips and the, you know, the crackers and the cookies and all this kind of stuff is huge.
And those sections are going to get really small.
The cookie and cracker and potato chip sections are just going to shrink and the fresh stuff is going to take over.
Because once you get in the habit of only eating the raw, healthy food, you don't crave that other stuff at all, you know.
I mean, if you have it once, then you'll, like, if you had it, like, last night, the next diet, you'll be really excited to, you know.
You'll be craving it again.
But if it's been a few days, you won't.
And, you know, you'll just, and you feel a lot better and a lot more energy.
So, I think people will be, a lot more vegetarians, people will be getting their protein out of just eggs and, like, whey protein powder.
But, but not soy powder.
Soy is, has too much, like, estrogen and stuff in it.
But there'll be a little bit of that.
I think whey is the best protein.
It comes from eggs.
And, but beef is going to go down a lot.
We subsidize our beef a lot.
I don't know how much it is, but it's a lot.
And it's way cheaper than it should be.
And it's really expensive, you know, a cow, one cow takes a whole acre just to grow him and, you know, their, all their farting and stuff.
It contributes to global warming and, and what you have to grow to feed them is a lot.
So that, they use pesticides for that.
And, anyways, we won't, we won't be, once we transparentize our political system, we'll find out how much money we actually subsidize the USDA.
And then we won't, we won't want to do that anymore.
And so beef will get really expensive.
And people will realize that, you know, it's, you know, it's not worth it to spend all that much money on healthily grown beef.
You know, it'll just be something you eat really on a special occasion.
So that section will get really small too.
So we'll start to bring back a lot of the exotic vegetables and fruits.
We've lost a lot of them over the last couple hundred years in America.
But, you know, if you go to other parts of the world, they've got all sorts of wacky wild vegetables.
And we'll bring those back.
We'll have a very wide range of vegetables from all over the world, all over the world, starting in the first world countries, you know.
And, yeah, I think the change will be, you know, 15 to 45 years in the future.
And then, you know, we'll be super fit after that.
So that's what food will be like in the future, 15 to 45 years.
Okay, peace out.
Peace out.
